title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 1)
text:
COS-FIELD ISA
2023-2024 Program Review
Submitted By:
Samantha Worthington, FIELD Director
College of the Siskiyous
800 College Avenue
Weed, CA 96094
December 9, 2024

-- 1 of 13 --

1. Program Description:
Since 2012, the College of the Siskiyous has maintained a partnership with
Farmworker Institute for Education and Leadership Development (FIELD)
through an Instructional Service Agreement (ISA). The annual agreement
entered is that FIELD provides instructional and other services for non-credit
English as a Second Language (ESL) courses and potentially other non-credit
courses and COS provides administrative and academic supervision of
instruction carried out by FIELD employees, record keeping, and other
administrative services pertaining to course offerings. The regulations pertaining
to ISAs are contained in California Education Code sections 78015, 84752 and
California Code of Regulations, title 5, sections 51006, 53410, 55002, 55003,
55005, 55300, 58050, 58051(c)–(g), 58051.5, 58055, 58056, 58058(b), 58102-
58106, 58108
Founded by Cesar Chavez in 1978, FIELD promotes economic and social
prosperity for underserved communities through education, workforce training,
and economic development. FIELD offers a wide range of free services “through
adult education, job training, career development – all of which leads to self-
confidence, self-sufficiency, and opportunities to break generational barriers,” an
October 2024 FIELD board member newsletter stated. ”We are proud that we
can help lift up our community and serve them in a multitude of ways.” FIELD’s
work and mission is consistent with the College’s mission to transform students’
lives through lifelong learning and success and helps broaden the socio-
economic strata it serves by offering ESL classes to significantly underserved
populations.
COS faculty develop curriculum for the courses. The College collects
apportionment on all the classes offered by FIELD and reimburses FIELD
throughout the year based on a mutually agreed upon rate per hour of
instruction. Since 2020, apportionment from these classes has not been collected
due to the California Community College Chancellor’s Office “hold harmless
“ rule in which colleges received a funding baseline. This regulation was enacted
to mitigate economic impacts from the pandemic.
The FIELD Director — a College employee — serves as the liaison between the
College and FIELD and does not oversee a specific academic program, rather
oversees programming related to the FIELD relationship.
The College’s partnership with FIELD enables the college to serve a significantly
more diverse student population and to reach and underserved population.
FIELD delivers its classes to economically disadvantaged adults over the age of
18 who are primarily Latino. In 2023-2024, the College’s partnership with FIELD
served more students than any other program at the College.

-- 2 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 2)
text:
Program Objectives
The 3-course ESL sequence is designed to take students from survival level
English skills to high intermediate English proficiency. Classes prepare students
for a variety of vocational or career fields and/or for transition into credit-bearing
college courses. Students who complete all 3 ESL courses and demonstrate
mastery of learning outcomes, earn a Certificate of Completion.
The Certificate of Completion provides “communicative English skills in speaking,
listening, reading, and writing to speakers of other languages.” The Certificate of
Completion is designed to meet the needs of diverse language learners and to
provide opportunity for students to learn at their own pace through class
repeatability.
New Course Modality -- Online ESL Classes:
The 2023-2024 academic year marked a new shift in ESL offerings with online
ESL classes offered through FIELD the entire year. Previously, there was one
session of online ESL held in April 2022. All online classes are 6 weeks.
Online ESL launched in April 2022
While face-to-face ESL classes were 90 hours in duration, online ESL classes
are 132 hours to account for additional instructor contact hours and student work
outside of class.
ESL 0561: ESL Level 1 (0 units, 132 total hours: 90 instruction hours, 12
instructor contact hours, 30 out of class hours)
ESL 0562: ESL Level 2 (0 units,132 total hours: 90 instruction hours, 12
instructor contact hours, 30 out of class hours)
ESL 0563: ESL Level 3 (0 units, 132 total hours: 90 instruction hours, 12
instructor contact hours, 30 out of class hours)
These online classes came with new requirements for FIELD regarding student
support and teaching requirements for instructors. These classes are offered
through Canvas and offered at 3 different levels through 3 different course shells.
The College requires FIELD to have Student Support Centers to support ESL
students and provide technological access required for online classes. Student
Support Centers are a place students go to get help with technological support
provided by teaching assistants. With the understanding that most FIELD
students have limited computer skills, teaching assistants can help with such
tasks as logging into class. There are 10-15 laptops in each center. Most of the
centers are at existing FIELD locations where its adult charter high school, EPIC,
is based.

-- 3 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 3)
text:
Instructors provide support via email and Zoom. The ESL instructor initially sends
out a welcome email to the student with all necessary information including
Canvas login. If the student has difficulties logging in or has questions the ESL
instructor tries to help and explain how to log in and navigate through the
platform. If this is still unclear, the student can go to a Student Support Center
where the tutors assist students with this or any other questions they might have.
The labs are available for the students while the classes are in session for the
student's convenience.
The Director ensures all instructors are certified to teach online in compliance
with the College’s requirements and that all instructors stay up-to-date with
training requirements. In addition, instructors must send documentation of the
requirement before being offered the course. These requirements help ensure
instructors have current knowledge of state and national accessibility
requirements and are aware of Title V guidelines for regular effective contact.
To ensure classes are consistently available to FIELD’s instructors, FIELD staff
developed “Introduction to Asynchronous Online Teaching” class, a 40-hour
training program, which meets the aforementioned requirements.
In the previous four years classes were offered in various locations including
Kern, Monterey, and Los Angeles counties. Classes were brought to students by
holding classrooms in neighborhood community centers, schools, and churches.
The FIELD ISA bolsters and broadens the College's ability to serve underserved
and a more diverse student body. This was particularly significant factor for the
College in 2023-2024 as the COS-FIELD partnership served more students than
another other program. Also, these ESL courses serve as an important pathway
for FIELD as many of its students continue on to its high school (mentioned
below) to pursue high school diplomas. Founded by Cesar Chavez in 1978,
FIELD promotes economic and social prosperity for underserved communities
through education, workforce training, and economic development. FIELD
delivers its classes to economically disadvantaged adults over the age of 18 who
are primarily Latino. According to FIELD, farmworkers’ education averages at a
6 th grade level and low literacy rates are high (as high as 21%).
Demographics
In the last four years, approximately 99% of FIELD students were Hispanic,
which is the same ethnicity percentage as the previous four years. Less than 1%
were Asian in the last four years.

-- 4 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 4)
text:
Gender
Generally, there are double the number of females in ESL classes to males. In
the last 4 years, 73% of students were females and 27% were males.
Age Chart
ESL students have a significant percentage of older students. In the last 4 years,
82% of ESL students were age 30 or older. The largest age group — 27% —
was ages 40 to 49. The smallest age group was ages 19 or younger at less than
2%.
Number of Students Served
The 2023-2024 COS-FIELD partnership served the most significant population of
students with the highest FTES percentage share of the College’s total FTES.
The 2023-2024 FTES were similar to 2020-2021 with the most drastic decline
occurring from 2021-2022 to 2022-2023. This was due to FIELD halting ESL
offerings for almost one year and in 2022-2023, there was only one 6-week
session on ESL, which was the first online ESL course offered. Please see charts
below for details. The second lowest enrollment in 2021-2022 was due to EPIC’s

-- 5 of 13 --

low enrollment as post-pandemic most students learned they preferred online
learning for its flexibility, however the high school was limited to how much
teaching could be offered online. This factor impacted the high school’s
enrollment and so FIELD decreased offerings, which eventually came to a halt in
2022-2023.
Enrollment by Year

-- 6 of 13 --

Enrollment by ESL Level
*(students can potentially enroll multiples times in the same course)
2. External Factors with Significant Impact:
Part-Time Faculty
All ESL faculty are the employees of FIELD. The College doesn’t have any
control over whether FIELD hires instructors full-time or part-time. Before FIELD
started offering online classes, leaders indicated they intended to create some
full-time teaching positions, however this has not happened. The Director
believes it would be more productive and beneficial to the students to have at
least one full-time instructor. A full-time instructor would help provide continuity
and stability as FIELD would be more reluctant to cancel all classes with short
notice.
Lack of Technology
Lack of computer access and stable internet is repeatedly relayed through SLO
surveys is students’ lack of computer access and stable internet access despite
Student Support Centers. Lack of computer knowledge is an issue as well since
all classes in 23-24 were online. In addition, many students have little experience
using computers.

-- 7 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 5)
text:
Student Education Levels
As FIELD recruits low-skilled workers — predominantly farmworkers — for its
ESL classes, low-education levels and literacy rates are always in issue within
the ESL student populations. This poses a unique set of challenges to ESL
instructors, challenges that should always be factored in when developing new
efforts.
Instructor Turnover
Due to budgetary constraints, FIELD pays its instructors less than the average
Adult Education hourly wage so the pay is not competitive. Also, instructors are
generally expected to work year round with significantly less breaks than a
teacher or college instructor.
Attendance Accounting Method for Online Classes
Online classes require the Chancellor’s Office census-based attendance
accounting rather than tracking student attendance. This has posed challenges
both for FIELD and College staff predominantly while enrolling students. As ESL
classes have a very short duration – 6 weeks – the first census usually comes
about 7 days after the class start dates. Currently, the College hand processes
paper registrations each session, which can be very timely. The Director is
working with Enrollment Services to help streamline the process and ensure
FIELD submits registration forms in ample time for them to be processed by the
first census. The hope is that the process will become smoother in 2025 both for
the College and FIELD
3. Progress on Outcome Assessment:
Student Learning Outcome Surveys
The Director collects SLO surveys after each 6-week session using Microsoft
Forms and stored digitally. This information is not on Elumen as was anticipated
and projected in the 2020 Program Review. The data helps inform the
Director how to better support ESL students and FIELD instructors.
CASAS Testing
The Director also collects the annual results of testing FIELD conducts in all its
ESL classes called the Comprehensive Adult Student Assessment System
(CASAS), which provides testing of basic and academic skills for youth and
adults and curriculum tools to design instruction. Every new ESL student is given
an appraisal test, and then a Pre-Test based on that appraisal. All students who
have taken a pre-test and who have attended a minimum of 40 hours of
instruction then take the post-test at the end of that session. This is repeated for
every session. Once a student takes the initial pre-test, he or she then only
takes the end of session post test from that time on regardless of how may

-- 8 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 6)
text:
subsequent sessions the student attends. Below is the CASAS data for the last 4
years.
Program/Course or Instructional Improvements Based on Assessment: The
Director works closely with FIELD staff to ensure quality online non-credit ESL
and sufficient oversight by accessing course shells and instructor
communications. In April 2023, the Director visited Student Support Centers to
ensure students had ample access to computers and staff support.
Program Assessment Plan for Next 4 Years: The Director will continue to have
instructors report SLOs after each session, which will continue to be collected
and stored digitally. Annual CASAS testing data will continue to be collected. The
Director consistently monitors the CASAS website for the 2023-2024 assessment
outcomes and determined they likely won’t be available until January 2025.
4. Qualitative Results: Course Completion & CASAS Assessments:
Course Completion Rates: Course completion rates in this section pertain the
number of students who transition from one ESL level to the next level and the
number of students who earned a Certificate of Completion. With only one full
year of online ESL and 23-24 CASAS Assessment Data unavailable at this time,
it is difficult to compare course completion rates from previous years in which
classes were taught in-person. The Director will revisit this data once the 2023-
2024 assessment outcomes are available through CASAS.
In 2023-2024, 23% of ESL students moved from ESL 1 to ESL 2 and 16% of
students moved from ESL 2 to ESL 3.

-- 9 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 7)
text:
Comparison of Certificates Earned
In 2021-2022, 4% of students --- 28 out of 701 students --- completed all 3 ESL
levels and earned Certificates of Completion. In 2023-2024, 3% of students -- 15
out of 582 students -- earned certificates.
2020-2021 2021-2022 2022-2023* 2023-2024
Certificates
of
Completion 18 28 0 15
*No opportunity existed in 2022-2023 to earn a Certificate of Completion as there was only one
session.
Discussion on Course Completion
With only one full year of online ESL and 23-24 CASAS Assessment Data
unavailable at this time, it is difficult at this point to compare course completion
rates from previous years in which classes were taught in-person. However, the
Director analyzed the number of students moving to higher ESL levels and
Certificates of Completion.
Number of students completing the sequence (ESL 1, 2 & 3)
In 2023-2024, approximately 3% of students completed all 3 ESL levels to earn
a Certificate of Completion, which was a 1% decrease of certificates earned in
2021-2022 and 2020-2021. While the distinguishing difference between 2023-
2024 and previous years are online classes, it is difficult to attribute to decrease
to teaching mode. The Director will revisit this data once the 2023-2024
assessment outcomes are available on the CASAS website. CASAS date from
previous years is cited below.
Course Completion Rate Goal
It is not in the Director’s purview to set Course Completion Rate goals, however
the Director consistently collaborates with FIELD to ensure program
improvement. Unfortunately, the CASAS Assessment data for 2023-2024 won’t
be available until January 2025 so the Director is unable to compare outcomes
for in-person classes to online classes.
Course Success Rate
To understand student success, the CASAS data is a far more meaningful data
regarding course success as proficiency in English speaking and writing skills are
assessed, which CASAS terms “Educational Functioning Level Completion
Rates” which pertains to those students who attend more than 40 hours of

-- 10 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 8)
text:
instruction within the online session and have taken a pre-test and post-test at
session’s end.
CASAS Educational Functioning Student Percentages
*The 2023-2024 CASAS assessment data is currently unavailable. Generally this
data is available in January or February or each academic year. The Director
consistently monitors the CASAS site for this information as eagerly awaits this
specific year’s data as is it the first full year offering online ESL.
5. Evaluation – Progress on Prior Goals:
Student Support Centers
During the launch of online ESL classes, the Director ensured FIELD established
Student Support Centers to support ESL students and provide technological
access required for online classes. FIELD Student Support Centers are places
students go to get help with technological support provided by teaching
assistants. With the understanding that most FIELD students have limited
computer skills, teaching assistants can help with such tasks as logging into
class. There are 10-15 laptops in each center. Most of the centers are at existing
FIELD locations where its adult charter high schools, EPIC, are based.
Class Visitations
The Director visits online classes by accessing all Canvas course shells and
finding opportunities to attend Zoom meetings. In April 2024, the Director visited
Student Support Centers in Yuba City, Greenfield, Merced, Hollister, and Paso
Robles, and met ESL Tutors and Site Coordinators.
Instructor Evaluations
The Director has ensured that instructor evaluations – peer, self, and student
evaluations -- continued with the online teaching environment.
Syllabi
The Director will continue to ensure syllabi that that meet accreditation standards
and confirms they are posted within all Canvas Course Shells.

-- 11 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 9)
text:
6. Closing the Budget Loop: The FIELD budget is predominantly set to cover
projected instruction reimbursement costs so this section is not very relevant.
The Director did not submit any Resource Allocation Requests in the last budget
cycle.
7. Vision:
This section is not technically applicable as the COS-FIELD ISA is not a
“program.” However, the Director plans to ensure all systems are in place to
ensure student success and compliance with all Title 5 requirements pertaining
to the ISA.
For this Vision section, the Director composed a “wish list” for the future of
FIELD’s ESL operations below. This Director believes this wish list will boost ESL
learning outcomes overall and number of students earning the ESL Certificate of
Completion.
Consistent ESL Offerings
In the last 4 years, FIELD has cancelled classes for several months and in 2022-
2023 there was only one session. The impacts on students and instructors are
significant as students lose their classes and instructors lose their jobs.
Chromebooks and Internet Access for All
Instructors consistently report on SLOs that lack of internet access and access to
computers continues to be an issue despite Student Support Centers. Instructors
also report that many students don’t know how to use computers sufficiently.
One Full-Time Instructor
The Director believes it would be more productive and beneficial to the students
to have at least one full-time instructor.
Launch Workplace English Online Class
The Director believes offering a “Workplace Readiness English” class would
support upper-level ESL students, complement FIELD’s adult charter high
school, and benefit students’ potential to obtain better jobs. More details about
the class can be found in the section below.

-- 12 of 13 --

title: 2024FIELDProgramReview (1) (Part 10)
text:
8. Four-Year Action Plan:
In the next four years, the Director will continue to serve as a liaison between
FIELD and the College to ensure compliance with Title 5 requirements. The
Director will also continue to ensure quality online ESL classes for online classes.
The Director will also help streamline the Registration process by creating a
guide for FIELD staff and providing training on the Guide to FIELD staff.
To better serve students with higher ESL proficiency, the Director will encourage
FIELD to start a class for students who earn the Certificate of Completion by
offering Workplace Readiness English. WORK 0502: Workplace Readiness
English is approved to be offered online. As the Course Outline states: “Students
will, through the development of written communication skills, strengthen
business vocabulary, learn proper sentence structure and correct spelling, proper
use of pronouns, and gain knowledge of online office reference materials. These
skills place an emphasis on job-specific skills in the workforce.” The Director
believes this class would support students and complement FIELD’s adult charter
high school.
The Director will also collaborate with FIELD to work on addressing two top
concerns: lack of technological access and instructor turnover.
9. Conclusions:
The Director primarily attributes the program’s weaknesses to low-education and
literacy levels of students, instructor turnover, technological challenges, and the
daily challenges students face in terms of work-home obligations. These
weaknesses can be seen in low percentages of students moving from one ESL
level to another and also earning Certificates of Completion.
While there are some inherent obstacles to serving ESL students, the Director is
hopeful that program changes can be made to better serve students.
Suggestions include hiring one-full time ESL instructor to ensure continuation of
course offerings, better address technological challenges by providing students
with more support and ensuring viable and accessible Student Support Centers.
The College’s partnership with FIELD enables the college to serve a significantly
more diverse student population and to reach an underserved population that it
otherwise would likely not be able to reach. In 2023-2024, the College’s
partnership with FIELD served more students than any other program at the
College. The relationship enables the College to better support equity
populations, specifically Latino students. ESL is a vital bridge to FIELD’s high
school and also improves students’ lives overall as it enables them to better
navigate daily life. Ultimately the College’s partnership with FIELD furthers COS’
mission to transform students’ lives through lifelong learning and success.

-- 13 of 13 --
